Comptroller General of the United States shall submit to the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs and the House Committee on Financial Services a report on\u2014\n**(A)**\nthe impact of the ownership by large institutional investors of single-family homes on housing availability and affordability for renters and homebuyers; and\n**(B)**\nthe effectiveness of this section in reducing demand by large institutional investors for single-family homes and expanding homeownership for renters and homebuyers.\n**(2) HUD report**\nNot later than 2 years after the date on which the prohibition under subsection (b)(1) takes effect, and again not later than 10 years after that date, the Secretary of the Housing and Urban Development, in consultation with the Secretary of the Treasury, the Administrator of the Rural Housing Service, the Executive Director of the Loan Guaranty Service of the Department of Veterans Affairs, the Chair of Securities and Exchange Commission, and the Director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, shall submit to the Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs of the Senate and the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives a report on\u2014\n**(A)**\nwhether there should be adjustments to the definition of the term large institutional investor ;\n**(B)**\nthe financial impact of this section on large institutional investors, renters, and homebuyers; and\n**(C)**\nany legislative recommendations regarding ways to improve the authorities provided under this section to increase the supply and affordability of single-family homes for purchase by individual homebuyers.\n**(3) Sense of Congress**\nIt is the sense of Congress that\u2014\n**(A)**\nthis section is intended to expand the number of single-family homes available to individuals for purchase and is aimed at preserving and expanding the supply of single-family homes available to individuals; and\n**(B)**\nany further study on the effectiveness of this section and any legislative recommendations therefrom should consider this sense of Congress.\n##### (f) Effective date\nThe requirements and prohibitions under subsections (b), (c), and (d) of this section\u2014\n**(1)**\nshall take effect on the date that is 180 days after the date of enactment of this Act; and\n**(2)**\nare repealed on the date that is 15 years after the effective date under paragraph (1).\nX\nCentral bank digital currency\n#### 1001. Central bank digital currency\nThe Federal Reserve Act ( 12 U.S.C. 221 et seq. ) is amended by inserting after section 16 ( 12 U.S.C. 411 et seq. ) the following:\n16A. Central bank digital currency (a) Definitions In this section: (1) Central bank digital currency The term central bank digital currency means a digital asset that\u2014 (A) is denominated in United States dollars; (B) is a United States currency; (C) is a direct liability of the Federal Reserve System; and (D) is widely available to the general public. (2) Digital asset The term digital asset has the meaning given the term in section 2 of the GENIUS Act ( 12 U.S.C. 5901 ). (b) Prohibition Except as provided in subsection (c), the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System or a Federal reserve bank may not issue or create a central bank digital currency or any digital asset that is substantially similar to a central bank digital currency directly or indirectly through a financial institution or other intermediary. (c) Exception Subsection (b) shall not prohibit any dollar-denominated currency that is open, permissionless, and private, and fully preserves the privacy protections of United States coins and physical currency. (d) Sunset This provisions of this section shall cease to be effective on December 31, 2030. .\nXI\nMiscellaneous\n#### 1101. Severability\nIf any provision of this Act, or the application thereof to any person or circumstance, is held invalid, the remainder of the Act, and the application of such provisions to other persons or circumstances, shall not be affected thereby.\n#### 1102. No additional funds authorized\nNo additional funds are authorized to be appropriated to carry out the requirements of this Act or any amendment made by this Act.",
```json
      "versionDate": "2026-03-12",
      "versionType": "EAS"
    }
  ]
}
```

## API Data: summaries

```json
{
  "govinfoBulk": {
    "introducedDate": "2026-01-15",
    "originChamber": "House",
    "payload": {
      "dublinCore": {
        "contributor": "Congressional Research Service, Library of Congress",
        "description": "This file contains bill summaries for federal legislation. A bill summary describes the most significant provisions of a piece of legislation and details the effects the legislative text may have on current law and federal programs. Bill summaries are authored by the Congressional Research Service (CRS) of the Library of Congress. As stated in Public Law 91-510 (2 USC 166 (d)(6)), one of the duties of CRS is \"to prepare summaries and digests of bills and resolutions of a public general nature introduced in the Senate or House of Representatives\". For more information, refer to the User Guide that accompanies this file.",
        "format": "text/xml",
        "language": "EN",
        "rights": "Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain."
      },
      "item": {
        "@attributes": {
          "congress": "119",
          "measure-id": "id119hr6644",
          "measure-number": "6644",
          "measure-type": "hr",
          "orig-publish-date": "2026-01-15",
          "originChamber": "HOUSE",
          "update-date": "2026-02-09"
        },
        "summary": {
          "@attributes": {
            "currentChamber": "HOUSE",
            "summary-id": "id119hr6644v07",
            "update-date": "2026-02-09"
          },
          "action-date": "2026-01-15",
          "action-desc": "Reported to House",
          "summary-text": "<p><strong>Housing for the 21st Century Act<br/><br/></strong>This bill revises federal housing programs, including by expanding available financing for affordable housing and providing grants for planning and community development activities.<br/><br/>For example, the bill increases the statutory maximum loan limits for mortgage insurance programs administered by the\u00a0Federal Housing Administration\u00a0for multifamily homes and requires the use of a more specific inflation index for such loans.<br/><br/>The bill also increases the maximum\u00a0eligible income for the Department of Housing and Urban Development's (HUD's) HOME Investment Partnerships Program (grants to states and localities to support housing for low-income households) and\u00a0establishes a grant program to assist regional, state, and local entities with\u00a0strategies to support affordable housing.<br/><br/>In addition, the bill</p><ul><li>exempts certain housing-related activities from the environmental review process, including certain construction, improvement, or rehabilitation of residential buildings;</li><li>excludes veterans' disability benefits from being considered as income for purposes of determining eligibility for the Veterans Affairs Supportive Housing (VASH) program;</li><li>establishes a pilot program to provide grants to public housing agencies (PHAs) and other owners of federally assisted housing to test the efficacy of temperature sensors to support compliance with temperature requirements;</li><li>eliminates the requirement that manufactured homes must be constructed with a permanent chassis; and</li><li>authorizes HUD to conduct performance reviews of organizations that\u00a0provide\u00a0housing counseling services.</li></ul><p>The bill also expands oversight of HUD and PHAs, such as by requiring PHAs to post information about contracts on their websites.</p><p>For more information about this bill, see <a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">CRS Report\u00a0</a><a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">R48849</a>.\u00a0</p>"
        },
        "title": "Housing for the 21st Century Act"
      }
    },
    "sourceUrl": "https://www.govinfo.gov/bulkdata/BILLSUM/119/hr/BILLSUM-119hr6644.xml",
    "summary": {
      "actionDate": "2026-01-15",
      "actionDesc": "Reported to House",
      "text": "<p><strong>Housing for the 21st Century Act<br/><br/></strong>This bill revises federal housing programs, including by expanding available financing for affordable housing and providing grants for planning and community development activities.<br/><br/>For example, the bill increases the statutory maximum loan limits for mortgage insurance programs administered by the\u00a0Federal Housing Administration\u00a0for multifamily homes and requires the use of a more specific inflation index for such loans.<br/><br/>The bill also increases the maximum\u00a0eligible income for the Department of Housing and Urban Development's (HUD's) HOME Investment Partnerships Program (grants to states and localities to support housing for low-income households) and\u00a0establishes a grant program to assist regional, state, and local entities with\u00a0strategies to support affordable housing.<br/><br/>In addition, the bill</p><ul><li>exempts certain housing-related activities from the environmental review process, including certain construction, improvement, or rehabilitation of residential buildings;</li><li>excludes veterans' disability benefits from being considered as income for purposes of determining eligibility for the Veterans Affairs Supportive Housing (VASH) program;</li><li>establishes a pilot program to provide grants to public housing agencies (PHAs) and other owners of federally assisted housing to test the efficacy of temperature sensors to support compliance with temperature requirements;</li><li>eliminates the requirement that manufactured homes must be constructed with a permanent chassis; and</li><li>authorizes HUD to conduct performance reviews of organizations that\u00a0provide\u00a0housing counseling services.</li></ul><p>The bill also expands oversight of HUD and PHAs, such as by requiring PHAs to post information about contracts on their websites.</p><p>For more information about this bill, see <a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">CRS Report\u00a0</a><a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">R48849</a>.\u00a0</p>",
      "updateDate": "2026-02-09",
      "versionCode": "id119hr6644"
    },
    "title": "Housing for the 21st Century Act"
  },
  "summaries": [
    {
      "actionDate": "2026-01-15",
      "actionDesc": "Reported to House",
      "text": "<p><strong>Housing for the 21st Century Act<br/><br/></strong>This bill revises federal housing programs, including by expanding available financing for affordable housing and providing grants for planning and community development activities.<br/><br/>For example, the bill increases the statutory maximum loan limits for mortgage insurance programs administered by the\u00a0Federal Housing Administration\u00a0for multifamily homes and requires the use of a more specific inflation index for such loans.<br/><br/>The bill also increases the maximum\u00a0eligible income for the Department of Housing and Urban Development's (HUD's) HOME Investment Partnerships Program (grants to states and localities to support housing for low-income households) and\u00a0establishes a grant program to assist regional, state, and local entities with\u00a0strategies to support affordable housing.<br/><br/>In addition, the bill</p><ul><li>exempts certain housing-related activities from the environmental review process, including certain construction, improvement, or rehabilitation of residential buildings;</li><li>excludes veterans' disability benefits from being considered as income for purposes of determining eligibility for the Veterans Affairs Supportive Housing (VASH) program;</li><li>establishes a pilot program to provide grants to public housing agencies (PHAs) and other owners of federally assisted housing to test the efficacy of temperature sensors to support compliance with temperature requirements;</li><li>eliminates the requirement that manufactured homes must be constructed with a permanent chassis; and</li><li>authorizes HUD to conduct performance reviews of organizations that\u00a0provide\u00a0housing counseling services.</li></ul><p>The bill also expands oversight of HUD and PHAs, such as by requiring PHAs to post information about contracts on their websites.</p><p>For more information about this bill, see <a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">CRS Report\u00a0</a><a href=\"https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48849#_Toc221206484\">R48849</a>.\u00a0</p>",
      "updateDate": "2026-02-09",
      "versionCode": "id119hr6644"
    }
  ]
}
```
